result:
The first time I understood that memory is a physical place, I was standing in the stairwell of a building that no longer exists.  It was 1993, the paint was the color of damp cigarette filters, and every fourth step had a rubber tread curled up like a chapped lip.  I had just learned to ride a bicycle, and the bicycle was gone—sold at a yard sale while I was at school—yet the ghost of its bell rang inside my mouth when I inhaled the stairwell’s smell of wet plaster and old soup.  The building was torn down in 2000 to make room for a pharmacy, but if I close my eyes I can still descend those stairs in real time, counting the thirteen steps between the second and first floor, feeling the banister wobble at the seventh.  Memory keeps the architecture; we simply become trespassers in our own former lives.

I used to think this was a kind of superpower, the way I could walk vanished corridors whenever I wanted.  Then I noticed the corollary: the living world presses bills and dentist appointments into my hands while I’m busy elsewhere, haunting a hallway that no longer has doors.  The past is a greedy landlord; it charges rent in the currency of attention.  Some days I pay until I am penniless in the present.

Yesterday the sky over the lake looked like a mistake—too turquoise, too theatrical, as if a child had taken over the controls.  I sat on the pier and watched a man in a red kayak paddle directly into the reflection of the sun, so that for a moment he rowed inside a column of fire.  He had no idea; from his vantage the water was merely bright.  I thought about yelling, “You’re inside the sun!” but language felt suddenly ridiculous, like trying to mail a teaspoon of seawater to someone in order to explain the ocean.  Instead I watched until the alignment broke apart and the column folded back into glitter.  The man kept paddling, unaware he had been briefly translated into myth.  I wondered how many myths I myself have blundered through while worrying about groceries.

When I was seven I believed every adult carried a hidden switch that, once flipped, would reveal the single correct way to live.  I searched for clues in the way my mother ironed pillowcases, in the way the mailman whistled twice before closing our mailbox, in the precise angle my teacher held her chin when she said “Nevertheless.”  I practiced possible futures in the mirror: chin up, chin down, eyebrows surprised, eyebrows stern.  Nothing clicked.  The mirror only showed me rehearsing.  One afternoon I asked my grandmother where the switch was hidden.  She was shelling peas into a colander, the green beads rattling like small applause.  Without looking up she said, “If I ever find it, I’ll let you know.  Until then, keep misbehaving.”  I thought she was teasing.  Now I think she was confessing.

The paradox of middle age is that the body becomes a daily newspaper delivered to your doorstep whether you subscribed or not: here is an ache you did not order, here is a new line between your eyebrows reporting on last night’s worries.  Meanwhile the mind becomes an attic where every box is mislabeled.  I open one marked “Winter 1987” and find the smell of my first CD player, the plastic jewel case cracking under my thumbnail, the disappointment of discovering that digital music can skip just like vinyl if you jump hard enough on the bedroom floor.  Another box, marked “Advice Never Taken,” contains only the sound of my father clearing his throat before saying, “You’ll understand when you’re older.”  I am older.  I do not understand.  The box is light enough to lift with one hand, yet I stagger under it.

Freud said we repeat what we do not remember, but he never mentioned that repetition can be pleasurable, like picking at a scab until it becomes a small moon crater on your knee.  I have watched myself choose the same flavor of heartbreak in different bodies, always convinced that this time the recipe will rise into something nourishing.  The brain is a superb chef of familiarity; it seasons the new with the spices of the old until the dish tastes like childhood, like home, like the very thing we swear we are fleeing.  We call that comfort, or destiny, or worst of all, “type.”  The truth is simpler: the tongue recognizes the recipe and relaxes.

Last winter I tried to interview my mother about her first memory.  We sat at the kitchen table while snow erased the backyard inch by inch.  She closed her eyes and said, “Heat rising off the road.  My barefoot on tar soft as chewing gum.  The smell of peaches from a stand that isn’t there anymore.”  Then she opened her eyes and asked if I remembered the name of the neighbor who used to return our trashcan to the porch each Thursday.  I said I did not.  She looked disappointed, as if I had carelessly misplaced a keepsake she had entrusted to me.  I wanted to argue that memory is not communal property, that forgetting is not malice, but the snow kept erasing the yard and I could already feel the conversation slipping through a hole in the afternoon.

There is a kind of prayer in typing, the way the keys click like rosary beads under the fingers.  I type to keep from disappearing, knowing full well that one day the hard drive will fail, the cloud will evaporate, some future me will drag this file into a trash icon that looks exactly like the metal bin we used to burn leaves in.  The ashes will be magnetic, invisible, everywhere.  Still, the typing is a tether, a way of saying I was here even while I am still here.  The paradox does not embarrass me.  Farmers plant pear trees they will never eat from; we call that hope, or husbandry, or the simple refusal to concede the future to strangers.

I have lately become obsessed with hourglasses, the way the sand measures nothing but its own falling.  Flip it and the future becomes past, but the middle—where the sand narrows into a throat—is always the present, that vanishing point we never quite occupy.  I bought one that is supposed to measure fifteen minutes, though I timed it and it runs closer to seventeen.  Those extra two minutes feel like embezzled time, cosmic coins I can spend in secret.  I flip it when I sit down to write, pretending the grains are words draining from a reservoir I will never exhaust.  When the last grain drops, I stop typing, even if I am mid-sentence.  The discipline pleases me; it reminds me that narratives, like lives, are allowed to end in places that feel arbitrary.

Sometimes I imagine the afterlife as a gigantic lost-and-found office.  Shelves reach the ceiling, stuffed with single mittens, plastic dinosaurs, wedding rings, and every hour I ever wasted scrolling through the lives of people I barely liked in high school.  A clerk—genderless, ageless, patient—hands me a slip and says, “Sign here for your collected boredom, your unlived Tuesdays.”  I will hoist the box and realize it weighs exactly as much as my body did at the moment of death.  Carrying it across the parking lot of eternity, I will hear the faint clink of hourglasses inside, all of them still running.

Until then, I keep misbehaving.  I paddle through reflections without recognizing the fire.  I trespass vanished stairwells.  I mislabel boxes, lose keepsakes, repeat heartbreaks, plant invisible pear trees.  The present keeps slipping through the throat of the hourglass, and I keep trying to catch it in my open mouth, tasting the metallic grit of maybe.  Tomorrow the lake might look ordinary again, the man in the red kayak nowhere in sight.  Nevertheless, I will sit on the pier and practice the art of useless attention: watching light fracture into pieces too bright to hold, naming each shard after a year I can still climb inside, even if the doors are gone.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
